title: Cleavage Under Targets and Release Using Nuclease (CUT&RUN)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"eekgj","text":"This Cleavage Under Targets and Release Using Nuclease (CUT\u0026RUN) protocol produces genomic occupancy data for a protein of interest in the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma brucei. The data produced is analyzed in a similar way as that produced by ChIP-seq. While we describe the protocol for parasites carrying an epitope tag for the protein of interest, antibodies against the native protein could be used for the same purpose. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":158,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"567sv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"deedi","text":"The last step contains a supplemental video with extra context and tips, as part of the protocols.io Spotlight series, featuring conversations with protocol authors.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":4,"length":9}],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"B564BFE2776A40838DD97C8C76019183","url":"https://www.protocols.io/view/cleavage-under-targets-and-release-using-nuclease-c7abzian?step=41"}}}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"1natk","text":"Guidelines","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f09pq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2tl3e","text":"Pay extra attention to time-sensitive incubation periods, particularly the DNA cleavage step. We find that working through the protocol rather speedily produces the best results, so don’t dawdle. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8307p","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"151r9","text":"Additional Notes","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8rgbc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b22ao","text":"We keep our centrifuges at   to make sure the samples don’t freeze if the temperature drifts slightly. All spin steps of the protocol can be performed anywhere between   and  .","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":27,"length":1},{"key":1,"offset":168,"length":1},{"key":2,"offset":174,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"4sdd4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fr8so","text":"While we have not rigorously tested whether increasing the exposure time to saponin influences permeabilization, we recommend that the saponin permeabilization step be completed in smaller batches of samples if large numbers of samples\n(\u003e10) are being processed simultaneously.\n","type":"align-left","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6j4n0","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"temperature","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"error_temperature":false,"guid":"56A019800F2A11EE9A613F45FCB05DC8","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"temperature":"10","unit":10}},"1":{"type":"temperature","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"error_temperature":false,"guid":"51C98B300F2A11EE9A613F45FCB05DC8","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"temperature":"4","unit":10}},"2":{"type":"temperature","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"error_temperature":false,"guid":"5396AA100F2A11EE9A613F45FCB05DC8","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"temperature":"10","unit":10}}}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"be4rm","text":"Before Start","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b4eq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f1k7m","text":"We use 50-75 million bloodstream parasites per sample. Parasites are cultured in HMI9 media with incubation at  and 5% CO2. Cultures should be prepared in advance so that sufficient numbers of parasites are available for each sample. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":121,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":111,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"atf06","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"11jok","text":"The protocol works best when everything is kept cold prior to cutting with the protein A-MNase fusion protein. We recommend keeping buffers chilled on ice and pre-cooling centrifuges to  . Protease inhibitors should be added to the NP-S buffer just before commencing the experiment. The amount of 2X Stop Buffer required for the experiment should be calculated and yeast spike-in DNA should be added prior to starting (see 2X Stop Buffer recipe below).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":297,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":186,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"8nerj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"temperature","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"error_temperature":false,"guid":"","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"temperature":"37","unit":10}},"1":{"type":"temperature","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"error_temperature":false,"guid":"5E1547800F2A11EE9A613F45FCB05DC8","id":0,"label":"","scientificNotation":false,"temperature":"4-10","unit":10}}}}

steps: Count parasite cultures with hemocytometer or other preferred counting method.
Spin down cells in centrifuge at 2800 x g, , 10 minutes . This spin step can be performed at Room temperature or at 10 °C .
If needed, combine samples from multiple Eppendorf tubes so that each final tube has 75 million cells, and spin again at 2800 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes in microcentrifuge. Remove supernatant.
Wash all samples with 1 mL NP-S Buffer with 0.1% Saponin.
Spin at 4600 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes . Remove sup.
Resuspend each sample in 100 µL NP-S Buffer with 0.1% Saponin.
Add EDTA to 2 millimolar (mM) final ( 4 µL of 0.05 Molarity (M) EDTA/ tube).

Add 5 µg α-HA antibody (or other antibody against protein of interest for experimental sample) OR add 5 µg α-H3 antibody (for control sample, might require titration) OR add 5 µg α-IgG antibody (for control sample).
Incubate for 00:45:00 on tube rotator at Room temperature .
Add 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent to each sample.
Spin at 4600 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes . Remove supernatant.
Add 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent to each sample.
Take out aliquot of 1 million cells for flow cytometry analysis (~ 14 µL if starting with 75 million cells). Set aside On ice .
Spin the remainder of the sample at 4600 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes . Remove supernatant. (total 2 washes).
Resuspend each sample in 100 µL of buffer NP-S Buffer no detergent.
Add 1.5 µL of pAG-MNase enzyme to each sample.
Incubate for 00:45:00 at Room temperature on rotator.
Add 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent to 1 million cell aliquot prepared above. We also routinely use HMI-9 media for the flow cytometry antibody incubation and washes.
Spin at 4600 x g, , 4 minutes .
Resuspend in 100 µL NP-S Buffer no detergent.
Stain with α-rabbit IgG PE at 1:200 for 00:15:00 at Room temperature .
Wash.
Resuspend in 300 µL NP-S Buffer no detergent (or HMI-9).
Transfer sample into flow cytometry tube.
Analyze on flow cytometer.
continued after 45m incubation above. Add 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent to each sample.
Spin at 4600 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes . Remove supernatant.
Add 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent to each sample.
Spin at 4600 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes . Remove supernatant.
Make sure to prepare enough 2X Stopbuffer with spike in control. This is the section where timing is very important.
Resuspend each sample in 100 µL buffer NP-S Buffer no detergent.
Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .
Add 2 µL 100 millimolar (mM) CaCl 2 to all samples (final concentration = 2mM), mix by flicking.
Incubate all samples at 25 °C for 00:05:00 . We recommend using a heat block as ambient temperature in the lab can vary.
Add 100 µL 2X Stop buffer to each sample and mix by flicking.
Incubate 00:10:00 at 37 °C to release CUT&RUN fragments from the insoluble nuclear chromatin.
Spin at 4600 x g, 10 °C, 4 minutes . Remove supernatant into new tube. SAVE SUPERNATANT.
To all samples add 2 µL of 10% SDS (final concentration = 0.1%), 3.3 µL of 10 mg/mL proteinase K (165µg/ml), and 1.33 µL of 1 mg/mL RNAse A (6.5µg/ml).
Mix by gentle flicking and incubate for 00:10:00 at 70 °C .
Purify using Ampure XP beads at 1.8X or phenol chloroform extraction. Following DNA purification, high-throughput sequencing libraries can be prepared using the preferred method of the research lab.
Remove supernatant and resuspend in small amount of remaining media (~ 100 µL ).
Wash in 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent (or HMI-9) at 7000 rpm, , 4 minutes . (1/2)
Wash in 1 mL NP-S Buffer no detergent (or HMI-9) at 7000 rpm, , 4 minutes .(2/2)
